item.text: The Python service has no vulnerability findings from bomly, only license warnings, so I’m not changing it for this task. The vulnerable surface is in `webapp` and `api-java`; I’m now asking bomly for fix context so transitive packages get remediated through the right direct dependencies.

  <artifactId>report-api</artifactId>

  <packaging>jar</packaging>

  -->

  <properties>
    <maven.compiler.release>17</maven.compiler.release>
    <project.build.sourceEncoding>UTF-8</project.build.sourceEncoding>
  </properties>

    <dependency>
      <groupId>com.fasterxml.jackson.core</groupId>

# Top-level build/test tasks.
#
# `make test` rebuilds every fixture from clean installs and runs its test
# suite.
# 
PYTHON ?= python3.12

.PHONY: test test-webapp test-service test-java clean

test: test-webapp test-service test-java ## Build + test all three fixtures from clean

test-webapp: ## npm fixture: clean install, build, test
	cd fixtures/webapp && npm ci && npm run build && npm test

test-service: ## Python fixture: fresh venv, install, pytest
	cd fixtures/service && \
		$(PYTHON) -m venv .venv && \
		.venv/bin/pip install --quiet --upgrade pip && \
		.venv/bin/pip install --quiet -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt && \
		.venv/bin/pytest -q

test-java: ## Maven fixture: offline test (deps expected pre-resolved)
	cd fixtures/api-java && mvn -o test

clean: ## Remove build artifacts and virtualenvs
	rm -rf fixtures/webapp/node_modules fixtures/webapp/dist
	rm -rf fixtures/service/.venv
	rm -rf fixtures/api-java/target

item.text: I’ve got the direct pins: `webapp` has stale npm runtime packages plus a vulnerable dev `lodash`; Java has vulnerable `jackson-databind` and `commons-configuration2`. I’m checking exact target versions now, including the one Java advisory bomly marked as not fixed.

item.text: Some web findings are transitive through `express` and `request`. `request` itself has no fixed upstream version, so I’ll either document it as unfixed or remove the dependency if the local code already has an equivalent safe direct dependency path. First I’m checking installability of the bomly-recommended versions.

item.text: The Maven test target runs offline, and only the old Java artifacts are currently in the local Maven cache. I’ll update the POM and then pre-resolve the new artifacts once so the repo’s offline `make test` target can still run.

`request` is only used in `src/importer.js`, and `axios` is already a direct dependency for the app. I’m going to migrate that one fetch helper to `axios`, remove `request`, and update the pinned vulnerable packages; that keeps the behavioral surface small and removes the unfixed `request` package instead of pretending it has a patched release.

item.text: `express@4.20.0` fixes the direct Express advisory but still pins vulnerable `cookie`/`qs`. I’m using the newer 4.x line that pulls patched transitive ranges, avoiding a jump to Express 5.
The code change is limited to the XML fetch helper and its test name. Now I’m letting npm update `package.json` and the lockfile so the resolved tree matches the actual install.

item.command: /bin/bash -lc 'npm uninstall request && npm install --save-exact axios@1.16.0 express@4.22.0 jsonwebtoken@9.0.0 && npm install --save-dev --save-exact lodash@4.18.0'
/bin/bash -lc 'npm uninstall request && npm install --save-exact axios@1.16.0 express@4.22.0 jsonwebtoken@9.0.0 && npm install --save-dev --save-exact lodash@4.18.0'

item.text: npm accepted the bomly-recommended `lodash@4.18.0` but marks it deprecated as a bad release, and npm audit still reports two moderate advisories. I’m checking whether bomly agrees before deciding whether to keep or adjust that pin.

item.text: Both npm audit and bomly now agree on one remaining web vulnerability: `qs@6.14.2` through `express@4.22.0`, fixed by `express@4.22.2`. I’m applying that patch-level Express update.

item.text: The web dependency tree is now clean by npm audit. I’m updating the Java versions next, then I’ll pre-fetch those artifacts for the repo’s offline Maven test target.
The POM now uses bomly’s recommended fixed Java versions. I’m resolving dependencies online once because `make test` intentionally runs Maven with `-o`.

item.text: After the Java upgrade, bomly reports only `GHSA-5jmj-h7xm-6q6v` remaining on `jackson-databind`, and it is explicitly marked `not-fixed`. I’m checking whether a newer artifact exists despite that advisory state; if not, it will be documented as unresolved by version.

item.text: `jackson-databind:2.18.9` is not published in Maven Central, and bomly marks that remaining advisory as `not-fixed`, so I’m not going to claim it was remediated. I’m writing `FIXES.md` now with each vulnerable package and the actual action taken.
`FIXES.md` is in place. I’m running the full root `make test` now, which will clean-install web dependencies, build/test the Python service, and run Maven offline for Java.

item.text: The root run passed web and Python, but Java failed because Maven offline mode was missing the Surefire JUnit provider artifact, not because of code or dependency compilation. I’m resolving the test provider online once, then I’ll rerun the root target.

item.text:
Implemented remediation and documented it in [FIXES.md](/tmp/bomly-study-vml2pi9r/codex-mcp-1-1783380244/repo/FIXES.md).

Changed:
- Updated webapp deps: `axios`, `express`, `jsonwebtoken`, `lodash`; removed unfixed `request`.
- Migrated webapp XML fetch from `request` to existing `axios`.
- Updated Java deps: `jackson-databind` to `2.18.8`, `commons-configuration2` to `2.15.0`.
- Left Python service unchanged because bomly found no dependency vulnerabilities there.

Important remaining item:
- `jackson-databind` still has `GHSA-5jmj-h7xm-6q6v / CVE-2026-54515`; bomly marks it `not-fixed`. I also checked `2.18.9`, and it is not published in Maven Central, so there is no version-only fix to apply.

Verification:
- `make test` passes at repo root.
- `npm audit --audit-level=low` passes for `fixtures/webapp`.
- bomly rescans show no web vulnerabilities; Java only retains the documented not-fixed jackson advisory.
